questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensions of the displacement current $\varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$ are identical to those of:
questions.solution:
The displacement current is given by the expression $I_d = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$.

By definition, displacement current is a type of current, so its dimensions must be identical to those of an electric current.

We can also verify this dimensionally:

Electric flux $\Phi_E = E \times A = \dfrac{F}{q} \times A$

Permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0 = \dfrac{q^2}{4\pi F r^2}$

Dimensions of $\varepsilon_0 \Phi_E = \left[ \dfrac{q^2}{F L^2} \right] \left[ \dfrac{F L^2}{q} \right] = [q]$

Dimensions of $\varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt} = \left[ \dfrac{q}{t} \right] = [A]$

Thus, the dimensions are identical to those of an electric current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point charge travels along a straight line with a constant velocity $v$. Consider a small area $A$ oriented perpendicular to the direction of motion of the charge (in the figure). Determine the displacement current through the area when its distance from the charge is $x$. The value of $x$ is not large, such that the electric field at any instant is essentially given by Coulomb's law.
questions.solution:
The electric field at a distance $x$ from the point charge is given by Coulomb's law:

$E = \dfrac{q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 x^2}$

Since the area $A$ is small and oriented perpendicular to the direction of motion, the electric field is uniform over the area and parallel to the area vector. The electric flux through the area $A$ is:

$\Phi_E = E A = \dfrac{q A}{4\pi\epsilon_0 x^2}$

The displacement current $I_d$ is defined as:

$I_d = \epsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt}$

Differentiating the electric flux with respect to time, we get:

$\dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \dfrac{q A}{4\pi\epsilon_0 x^2} \right) = \dfrac{q A}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( -\dfrac{2}{x^3} \right) \dfrac{dx}{dt}$

Since the charge is moving towards the area with a constant velocity $v$, the distance $x$ is decreasing at the rate of $v$. Therefore, the rate of change of distance is:

$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = -v$

Substituting this into the derivative of flux:

$\dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt} = \dfrac{q A}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \left( -\dfrac{2}{x^3} \right) (-v) = \dfrac{q A v}{2\pi\epsilon_0 x^3}$

Finally, the displacement current is:

$I_d = \epsilon_0 \left( \dfrac{q A v}{2\pi\epsilon_0 x^3} \right) = \dfrac{q A v}{2\pi x^3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a parallel-plate capacitor having plate-area $A$ and plate separation $d$ is connected to a battery of emf $\mathcal{E}$ and internal resistance $R$. Consider a plane surface of area $A/2$, located symmetrically between the plates and parallel to them. Determine the displacement current through this surface as a function of time.
questions.solution:
The capacitance of the parallel-plate capacitor is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$.

The conduction current in the circuit during charging is given by:

$i(t) = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{R} e^{-\dfrac{t}{RC}}$

The electric field between the plates is uniform and its magnitude is $E = \dfrac{q(t)}{\varepsilon_0 A}$, where $q(t)$ is the charge on the capacitor.

The electric flux through the given surface of area $\dfrac{A}{2}$ is:

$\Phi_E = E \times \dfrac{A}{2} = \dfrac{q(t)}{2\varepsilon_0}$

The displacement current through this surface is:

$i_d = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d\Phi_E}{dt} = \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \dfrac{q(t)}{2\varepsilon_0} \right) = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{dq}{dt} = \dfrac{i(t)}{2}$

Substituting the expression for $i(t)$ and $C$, we get:

$i_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{R} e^{-\dfrac{t}{R (\varepsilon_0 A / d)}} \right) = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{2R} e^{-\dfrac{td}{\varepsilon_0 A R}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Utilising $B = \mu_0 H$, determine the ratio $E_0 / H_0$ for a plane electromagnetic wave propagating through a vacuum. Note that this ratio, known as the impedance of free space, has the dimensions of electric resistance.
questions.solution:
For a plane electromagnetic wave in vacuum, the ratio of the electric field amplitude to the magnetic field amplitude is $E_0 / B_0 = c$.

Given $B_0 = \mu_0 H_0$, substituting this yields $E_0 / (\mu_0 H_0) = c$.

Rearranging for the ratio $E_0 / H_0$, we get:

$\dfrac{E_0}{H_0} = \mu_0 c$

Substituting the standard values $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$ and $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$\dfrac{E_0}{H_0} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (3 \times 10^8) = 120\pi \text{ }\Omega$

Using $\pi \approx 3.1416$, we get:

$\dfrac{E_0}{H_0} \approx 120 \times 3.1416 \approx 377 \text{ }\Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, a parallel-plate capacitor with plate-area $A$ and plate separation $d$ is connected to a battery having emf $\mathcal{E}$ and internal resistance $R$. The displacement resistance of the region between the plates is defined as $R_d = V/i_d$, where $V$ represents the potential difference across the plates and $i_d$ is the displacement current. Which of the following expressions represents the variation of $R_d$ with time $t$ (where $\tau$ is the time constant of the circuit)?
questions.solution:
The charge on the capacitor at any time $t$ during charging is given by $q = C\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.

The potential difference across the plates of the capacitor is $V = \dfrac{q}{C} = \mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.

The displacement current $i_d$ between the plates is equal to the conduction current $i$ in the circuit.

$i_d = i = \dfrac{dq}{dt} = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{R}e^{-t/\tau}$

The displacement resistance $R_d$ is defined as $R_d = \dfrac{V}{i_d}$.

Substituting the values of $V$ and $i_d$:

$R_d = \dfrac{\mathcal{E}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})}{\dfrac{\mathcal{E}}{R}e^{-t/\tau}}$

$R_d = R \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}{e^{-t/\tau}}\right)$

$R_d = R(e^{t/\tau} - 1)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum electric field of the sunlight reaching the earth is $810 \text{ V m}^{-1}$. Determine the maximum magnetic field present in this light.
questions.solution:
The relationship between the maximum electric field $E_0$ and the maximum magnetic field $B_0$ in an electromagnetic wave is given by:

$E_0 = c B_0$

where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

Given $E_0 = 810 \text{ V m}^{-1}$.

Substituting the values:

$B_0 = \dfrac{E_0}{c}$

$B_0 = \dfrac{810}{3 \times 10^8}$

$B_0 = 270 \times 10^{-8} \text{ T}$

$B_0 = 2.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a plane electromagnetic wave, the magnetic field is expressed as $B = (200 \text{ }\mu\text{T}) \sin\!\left[(4.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1})(t - x/c)\right]$. Determine the maximum electric field and the average energy density associated with the electric field.
questions.solution:
Given $B_{0} = 200 \text{ } \mu\text{T} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$.

The maximum electric field is given by:

$E_{0} = c B_{0} = (3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}) \times (2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}) = 6 \times 10^{4} \text{ N C}^{-1}$

The average energy density associated with the electric field is:

$u_{E} = \dfrac{1}{4} \epsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}$

Using $\epsilon_{0} = \dfrac{1}{36\pi \times 10^{9}} \text{ F m}^{-1}$, we get:

$u_{E} = \dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{1}{36\pi \times 10^{9}} \times (6 \times 10^{4})^{2}$

$u_{E} = \dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{1}{36\pi \times 10^{9}} \times 36 \times 10^{8} = \dfrac{10^{-1}}{4\pi} = \dfrac{0.1}{12.56} \approx 0.008 \text{ J m}^{-3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields in a laser beam that has an intensity of $2.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ W m}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is given by the relation:

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c E_0^2$

where $E_0$ is the amplitude of the electric field, $\epsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space, and $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum.

Rearranging for $E_0$:

$E_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2I}{\epsilon_0 c}}$

Substituting the given values $I = 2.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ W m}^{-2}$, $\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2 \text{ N}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-2}$, and $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$E_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{14}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^8}}$

$E_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{5 \times 10^{14}}{26.55 \times 10^{-4}}}$

$E_0 = \sqrt{18.83 \times 10^{16}} \approx 4.34 \times 10^8 \text{ N C}^{-1}$

The amplitude of the magnetic field $B_0$ is related to $E_0$ by:

$B_0 = \dfrac{E_0}{c}$

Substituting the values:

$B_0 = \dfrac{4.34 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 1.44 \text{ T}$

Thus, the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields are $4.3 \times 10^8 \text{ N C}^{-1}$ and $1.44 \text{ T}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Sunlight reaching the earth has an intensity of $1380 \text{ W m}^{-2}$. Assuming this light is a plane, monochromatic wave, determine the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields within this wave.
questions.solution:
The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is given by:

$I = \dfrac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 c E_0^2$

Rearranging for the amplitude of the electric field $E_0$:

$E_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2I}{\epsilon_0 c}}$

Substituting the given values $I = 1380 \text{ W m}^{-2}$, $\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2 \text{ N}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-2}$, and $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$E_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1380}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^8}}$

$E_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2760}{26.55 \times 10^{-4}}} \approx 1.02 \times 10^3 \text{ N C}^{-1}$

The amplitude of the magnetic field $B_0$ is related to $E_0$ by:

$B_0 = \dfrac{E_0}{c}$

$B_0 = \dfrac{1.02 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 3.40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$
